publicationStmt.p: Transcribed from Bodleian Library, Lovelace Byron collection, Box 166, folios 84-86. Transcription by Lisa Gold (2022). TEI conversion for Sextant pipeline, 2026.

div.p: Dearest W. We were so hurried yesterday, that neither the Hen nor I could give all the explanations requisite about the E. Noel correspondence.
My "proposed answer" (if it goes at all) would be in part superseded at any rate, by what the
Hen told you she had herself written to him last night, in order to save the necessity of my immediately replying further previously to receiving your wishes & advice on the subject.
I think the tone of his letters, & of Fanny's also, odious & insolent.--
I am glad you have retained De la Rive's letter for Crosse.
Your letters are to me the greatest pleasure.
You can scarcely estimate how much I think, & how anxiously I look for them.
I have been to Bath today, to look for accomodation, but found nothing I approved. The Hen is ill, & has had on leeches. An attack of the chest.
Altho' her strength is certainly vastly increased, yet I see symptoms on a more intimate observation, that I by no means like.
I do not think her life
is likely to be a very lengthened one. Still, with care, I trust 10 or even 15 years may be possible, if not probable.
Paralysis is the danger, I feel no doubt; but I think it would probably be of the kind which affects the motive powers only, & not the intellect.
Tell Mr. Crosse that, (great as is the disappointment to me), I think he has decided wisely both as regards you, & himself. Tell his son how I regret to miss
the opportunity of improving my acquaintance too, with himself.--
Beg Miss Boutell to write to me, & to tell me how she thinks the chidlren going on altogether. Tell Annabella I am pleased with her letter, & tell Byron to write.
